lessons.title: Introduction to Property Law
learning_objectives: Explain why a surveyor must understand the legal framework of property ownership, not just measurement.
Trace a Texas chain of title back to one of the four sovereigns of the soil.
Distinguish the surveyor's role (locating boundaries) from the attorney's role (opining on ownership).
key_takeaways: Property law defines who owns what; surveying locates where that ownership lies on the ground.
Every Texas title traces to Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, or the State of Texas.
Texas land was never federal public domain, so there is no PLSS township-range grid.

content.html:
Why Surveyors Study Property Law

A boundary survey is not merely a measurement exercise. The surveyor is asked to locate, on the ground, the limits of a legal interest in land created by deeds, grants, and statutes that may be centuries old. To do that competently you must understand how property rights are created, transferred, and described in writing. The measurement is the easy part; the hard part is deciding which evidence of a boundary the law treats as controlling.

Property law answers who owns what and what bundle of rights ownership includes. Surveying answers where that ownership lies. The two disciplines meet at the boundary line. A surveyor who ignores the legal hierarchy of evidence (monuments over distances, senior rights over junior) can produce a mathematically perfect survey that is legally wrong, and a legally wrong boundary is the most expensive mistake a surveyor can make.

The Bundle of Rights

Ownership of land is best understood as a bundle of rights rather than a single thing. The bundle includes the right to possess, to use, to exclude others, to transfer (sell, lease, gift, devise), and to profit from the land. These sticks can be separated: an owner may sell the surface but reserve the minerals, grant an easement (a right to use) without giving up possession, or lease the land while keeping the fee. In Texas the mineral estate is frequently severed from the surface and is, by long-standing rule, the dominant estate, meaning the mineral owner may use as much of the surface as is reasonably necessary to develop the minerals. A surveyor must know which sticks the survey is being asked to locate.

The Sovereign Source of Texas Title

Every parcel of privately held land in the United States traces backward through a chain of deeds to an original grant from a sovereign. In Texas that chain bottoms out in one of four sovereigns of the soil: Spain (1716–1821), Mexico (1821–1836), the Republic of Texas (1836–1845), and the State of Texas (1845–present). The instrument that first severs land from the sovereign is a Spanish or Mexican title or a Republic/State patent; every later deed runs from that first grantee.

This matters enormously for boundary work. Because Texas was an independent republic and retained its public lands at annexation in 1845, Texas land was never part of the federal public domain. There is no federal township-range Public Land Survey System (PLSS) grid here. Instead, Texas land is described by metes and bounds keyed to an original General Land Office (GLO) survey and abstract number. Nearly every distinctive feature of Texas boundary law flows from this single historical fact.

content.text: Texas differs: Unlike the federal public-land states (where title flows from the United States via the federal GLO/BLM and the PLSS grid), Texas title flows from the State. Texas has almost no federal land and no township-range sections. Do not try to read a Texas description in township/range terms.

content.html:
The Surveyor's Lane vs. the Attorney's Lane

A surveyor locates boundaries; a surveyor does not adjudicate ownership or render legal opinions on title. When you retrace a boundary you are reconstructing where the original line was run and marked, applying the legal rules of evidence (dignity of calls, footsteps of the original surveyor) to physical and record evidence. Deciding who owns a disputed strip, quieting title, or interpreting an ambiguous reservation is the work of a court and counsel. The professional skill is staying in your lane: report what the evidence shows the boundary to be, flag conflicts and gaps/overlaps, and let the legal questions go to the parties' attorneys. Practicing law without a license, or rendering a title opinion, exposes the surveyor to liability and discipline.

sections.title: Where boundary disputes are litigated in Texas
sections.content: Boundary and title disputes are civil matters. They begin in a Texas District Court (general jurisdiction over trespass-to-try-title and quiet-title actions), may be appealed to a regional Court of Appeals, and end at the Supreme Court of Texas. Texas is one of only two states (with Oklahoma) that splits its highest court in two: the Court of Criminal Appeals handles criminal cases and never touches boundary precedent.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Property law defines ownership; surveying locates it on the ground; they meet at the boundary line.
Ownership is a bundle of separable rights (possess, use, exclude, transfer, profit).
Every Texas title traces to one of four sovereigns: Spain, Mexico, the Republic, or the State.
Texas land was never federal public domain, so there is no PLSS grid; descriptions tie to a GLO survey and abstract number.
Surveyors locate boundaries and flag conflicts; they do not render title opinions.

flashcards.definition: The four sources of original Texas land title: Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, and the State of Texas. Every Texas chain of title runs back to one of them.
The concept that land ownership is a collection of separable rights: to possess, use, exclude, transfer, and profit. Individual sticks (e.g., minerals, easements) can be severed and conveyed.
The instrument by which the Republic or State of Texas first conveys (severs) land from the sovereign to a grantee. The Spanish/Mexican equivalent is a 'title.'

lessons.title: Types of Property (Real vs. Personal)
learning_objectives: Distinguish real property from personal property and explain why the distinction matters to a surveyor.
Apply the legal tests for whether an item is a fixture or remains personalty.
Identify appurtenances and explain how they pass with a conveyance of land.
key_takeaways: Real property is land and things permanently attached; personal property (chattels) is movable.
Fixtures (formerly chattels affixed to land) are determined by the MARIA-type tests of annexation, adaptation, and intent.
Appurtenant rights, such as easements, pass with the land even when not separately listed.

content.html:
Real Property vs. Personal Property

The law divides all property into two great classes. Real property (realty) is land and everything permanently attached to it — the soil itself, the rock and minerals beneath, the airspace above (within limits), and improvements such as buildings, fences, and growing trees. Personal property (personalty, or chattels) is everything else — movable items not permanently attached, from vehicles and equipment to harvested crops and a surveyor's total station.

The distinction governs how property is transferred (real property requires a written, signed instrument under the Statute of Frauds; personalty often does not), how it is taxed, how it descends at death, and what a boundary survey actually concerns. A surveyor locates real property boundaries. But the realty/personalty line can be surprisingly slippery, and it most often becomes contested at the boundary, the fence, the encroaching structure, or the crop on the disputed strip.

content.text: Texas differs: In Texas an oil-and-gas leasehold and the severed mineral estate are treated as interests in real property (an estate in land), not mere personal property. A conveyance of the surface does not carry minerals that were previously reserved.

content.html:
Fixtures: When a Chattel Becomes Realty

A fixture is an item that began as personal property but became so attached to land that the law now treats it as part of the realty — and it therefore passes with a conveyance of the land unless reserved. Whether a heater, a building, a sign, or an irrigation system is a fixture is decided by a three-factor test commonly summarized as annexation, adaptation, and intention:

  • Annexation — the manner and degree of physical attachment (bolted to a foundation vs. resting on the surface).
  • Adaptation — how well the item is suited to the use of the land (a furnace adapted to a house).
  • Intention — the objective intent of the party who annexed it, judged from the circumstances. Intent is the controlling factor.

The fixture question routinely surfaces in boundary work: a building footing, a retaining wall, or a pump house straddling a property line is realty and may constitute an encroachment, whereas a removable shed sitting on skids may not.

rows: A house on a slab foundation
A trade fixture installed by a tenant

content.html:
Appurtenances

An appurtenance is a right or thing that belongs to and passes with the land — literally, that which ‘runs with’ the realty. The classic example is an appurtenant easement: a right-of-way benefiting Parcel A across Parcel B is appurtenant to Parcel A and is conveyed automatically whenever Parcel A is sold, even if the deed never mentions it. Other appurtenances include water rights that attach to riparian land and the benefit of a recorded restrictive covenant. The rule for surveyors and title examiners: a deed conveys not only the described land but also ‘all appurtenances thereunto belonging.’ When mapping a parcel, you must research and depict appurtenant easements that benefit or burden the tract, because they travel with title whether or not the current deed recites them.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Real property is land and permanently attached improvements; personal property is movable chattels.
Fixtures are former chattels that became realty; the test is annexation, adaptation, and (controlling) intention.
In Texas, severed minerals and oil-and-gas leases are interests in real property.
Appurtenances such as appurtenant easements pass with the land automatically as part of any conveyance.

flashcards.definition: An item that was once personal property but is now so attached to and adapted to land that the law treats it as part of the realty. Tested by annexation, adaptation, and intention (intent controls).
A right or thing that belongs to and passes with the land, such as an appurtenant easement or attached water right. It conveys with the realty even if not separately listed in the deed.
Personal property; movable items not permanently attached to land. Generally transferable without the Statute-of-Frauds writing required for real property.

An article a tenant attaches to leased premises for use in a trade or business. By exception, the tenant may generally remove it before the lease ends rather than forfeiting it as a fixture to the landlord.

lessons.title: Estates and Interests in Land
learning_objectives: Distinguish freehold estates (fee simple, defeasible fees, life estate) from non-freehold (leasehold) estates.
Identify the future interests that accompany defeasible fees and life estates.
Apply the Texas presumption that a conveyance passes a fee simple unless a lesser estate is expressly limited.
key_takeaways: Fee simple absolute is the largest estate; defeasible fees and life estates are lesser freeholds.
Texas presumes a conveyance passes the entire (fee simple) estate unless a lesser estate is expressly limited (Prop. Code 5.001).
Texas abolished several archaic common-law rules (Shelley's Case, worthier title) by Prop. Code 5.042, giving effect to grantor intent.

content.html:
What Is an Estate in Land?

An estate is the degree, quantity, nature, and extent of a person's interest in land — chiefly, how long the interest lasts and what conditions attach to it. Estates fall into two families. Freehold estates are of uncertain or indefinite duration and carry seisin (ownership): the fee simple, defeasible fees, and the life estate. Non-freehold (leasehold) estates last for a fixed or determinable term and give possession without ownership of the fee: estates for years, periodic tenancies, and tenancies at will or at sufferance.

For a surveyor the estate question shapes who has standing to authorize and rely on a survey, who must sign a boundary agreement, and whose interest a future interest holder can later assert. A life tenant cannot unilaterally bind the remainderman to a boundary line; a tenant cannot convey the landlord's fee.

rows: "to A so long as / until / while"
Fee simple subject to condition subsequent
"to A, but if ... then grantor may re-enter"
Right of re-entry / power of termination (grantor)

content.html:
Fee Simple and Defeasible Fees

The fee simple absolute is the largest possible estate — ownership of potentially infinite duration with no conditions. The defeasible fees are fees that may be cut short on the happening of an event. A fee simple determinable ends automatically when a stated event occurs (‘so long as,’ ‘until,’ ‘while’), leaving the grantor a possibility of reverter. A fee simple subject to condition subsequent does not end automatically; instead it gives the grantor a right of re-entry (power of termination) that must be affirmatively exercised (‘but if,’ ‘provided that’). The distinction matters because the automatic-versus-elective difference controls when title actually shifts — a recurring issue when a deed restriction or a ‘school purposes only’ condition is litigated.

Life Estates and Future Interests

A life estate lasts for the duration of a measuring life (‘to A for life’); a variant, the life estate pur autre vie, is measured by a different person's life. The life tenant may use and possess the land but owes a duty not to commit waste — not to damage the property in a way that injures the future interest. When the life estate ends, the land passes either back to the grantor (a reversion) or onward to a third party (a remainder). A remainder is vested if it is given to an ascertained person with no condition precedent, and contingent if it depends on an uncertain event or an unascertained taker. These future interests are real, presently existing property rights even though possession is postponed — and the holder's consent may be required to fix a boundary or convey clear title.

The Texas Fee-Simple Presumption

Texas resolves doubt in favor of the larger estate. Under Texas Property Code §5.001, a conveyance of real property passes the entire estate the grantor owns — a fee simple — ‘unless the estate is limited by express words or unless a lesser estate is conveyed by construction or operation of law.’ The drafter who wants to convey something less than a fee must say so plainly. This presumption underlies most modern Texas deeds and explains why a simple ‘grant’ with no estate language nonetheless passes full ownership.

content.text: Texas differs: Texas Property Code 5.042 (eff. Jan. 1, 1984) abolished several archaic common-law estate rules, including the Rule in Shelley's Case, the doctrine of worthier title, and the rule forbidding a remainder to the grantor's heirs. Such limitations are now given effect according to the grantor's intent. (Note: 5.042 does NOT abolish destructibility of contingent remainders, which Texas rejects by case law instead.)

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Freehold estates (fee simple, defeasible fees, life estate) carry ownership; leaseholds give only term possession.
Fee simple determinable ends automatically (possibility of reverter); subject-to-condition-subsequent requires affirmative re-entry.
A life estate carries a no-waste duty and is followed by a reversion (grantor) or remainder (third party).
Texas presumes a conveyance passes a full fee simple unless a lesser estate is expressly limited (Prop. Code 5.001).
Prop. Code 5.042 abolished Shelley's Case, worthier title, and the remainder-to-grantor's-heirs rule, honoring grantor intent.

flashcards.definition: The largest estate in land: ownership of potentially infinite duration with no conditions or limitations. In Texas a conveyance is presumed to pass a fee simple unless a lesser estate is expressly limited (Prop. Code 5.001).

flashcards.term: Fee simple determinable vs. subject to condition subsequent
flashcards.definition: A determinable fee ends automatically on a stated event ('so long as / until'), leaving a possibility of reverter. A fee subject to condition subsequent ends only if the grantor affirmatively exercises a right of re-entry ('but if / provided that').
A freehold lasting for the duration of a measuring life. The life tenant may possess and use the land but must not commit waste that injures the following reversion or remainder.
flashcards.hint_1: Measured by a life, not a term of years.

flashcards.definition: Statute (eff. 1984) abolishing the Rule in Shelley's Case, the doctrine of worthier title, and the rule against a remainder to the grantor's heirs, directing that such limitations be given effect per the grantor's intent.

lessons.title: Legal Descriptions Overview
learning_objectives: Identify the major systems used to describe land legally and explain which apply in Texas.
Explain why Texas uses metes-and-bounds tied to a GLO original survey and abstract rather than the PLSS.
Recognize the components of an adequate legal description and the consequences of an inadequate one.
key_takeaways: Common legal-description systems are metes-and-bounds, lot-and-block (recorded plat), and the federal PLSS.
Texas uses metes-and-bounds keyed to an original GLO survey name and abstract number; it never adopted the PLSS.
A legal description must identify a single, locatable parcel; an inadequate description can void the conveyance under the Statute of Frauds.

content.html:
What a Legal Description Must Do

A legal description is the written portion of a deed or grant that identifies the land conveyed. To be legally sufficient under the Statute of Frauds (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §26.01), it must describe the land with enough certainty that the parcel can be identified to the exclusion of all other land — either from the writing itself or from other writings it references. A street address or a casual name is generally not a legal description. A description that is so vague that the tract cannot be located is fatally defective, and the conveyance can fail entirely. The surveyor and the title examiner therefore treat the description as the foundation of every boundary opinion.

The Three Major Description Systems

Metes and bounds describes the perimeter of a tract as a continuous series of calls — a beginning point, then bearing-and-distance courses and monument calls running around the boundary back to the point of beginning. It can describe any irregular shape and is the oldest system, used in the original colonies and throughout Texas.

Lot and block (the recorded-plat or ‘subdivision’ method) describes land by reference to a numbered lot within a numbered block of a subdivision plat recorded in the county records (‘Lot 7, Block 3, Sunset Acres, as recorded in…’). The plat itself carries the metes-and-bounds and monument detail; the deed simply incorporates it.

The Public Land Survey System (PLSS) is the federal rectangular system of townships, ranges, and sections used across most of the western United States. It does not exist in Texas.

content.text: Texas differs: Texas never adopted the federal PLSS. Because Texas retained its public lands at statehood, its land is described by metes-and-bounds tied to an original GLO survey (named for the grantee) and an abstract number, e.g., 'the John Doe Survey, Abstract No. 123.' There is no township/range/section. Do not attempt to read a Texas description in PLSS terms.

content.html:
The Texas Caption: Survey Name and Abstract Number

The spine of Texas land identification is the original land-grant survey and its abstract number. A Texas description almost always opens with a caption reciting the county, the original survey (named for the original grantee or railroad company that earned the grant), and the abstract number assigned by the GLO — for example, ‘situated in the H. & T.C.R.R. Co. Survey, Abstract No. ___, in ____ County, Texas.’ The point of beginning and at least one controlling corner are tied back to a corner of that original survey so the parcel can be relocated independently of the surveyor's own monuments. This abstract-based framework is the direct legacy of the GLO patent system and replaces the section/township anchor used in PLSS states.

content.alt: Comparison diagram of a metes-and-bounds tract, a lot-and-block plat, and a PLSS section grid
content.caption: Figure: Three legal-description systems compared. Texas uses metes-and-bounds keyed to a GLO survey/abstract (left and center); the PLSS grid (right) is not used in Texas.

sections.title: Components of an adequate metes-and-bounds description
sections.content:
• Caption — county, original GLO survey, abstract number, parent-tract reference, acreage.
  • Point of beginning (POB) — tied to a found, identifiable monument and/or an original survey corner.
  • Calls — bearing and distance for each course, with a stated basis of bearing.
  • Monument calls — natural and artificial monuments found or set along the perimeter.
  • Closure — the calls must mathematically return to the POB, forming a closed figure with no gap or overlap.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A legal description must identify a single, locatable parcel to satisfy the Statute of Frauds; a fatally vague description can void the conveyance.
The three major systems are metes-and-bounds, lot-and-block (recorded plat), and the federal PLSS.
Texas uses metes-and-bounds tied to a GLO survey name and abstract number; it has no PLSS township/range/section grid.
A Texas caption recites the original survey and abstract; the POB ties back to an original survey corner.

flashcards.definition: A legal-description method that traces the perimeter of a tract as a continuous series of bearing-and-distance courses and monument calls, beginning and ending at a point of beginning. The system used throughout Texas.

A number the GLO assigns to each original land-grant survey. Texas descriptions are keyed to the original survey name and its abstract number (e.g., 'Abstract No. 123') in place of a PLSS township/range/section.
A description by reference to a numbered lot in a numbered block of a recorded subdivision plat. The plat supplies the boundary detail; the deed incorporates it by reference.

lessons.title: Rules of Construction for Deeds
learning_objectives: State the dignity-of-calls hierarchy used to resolve conflicting calls in a description.
Explain that the hierarchy is a rebuttable rule of construction subordinate to the intent of the parties and the footsteps of the original surveyor.
Apply Stafford v. King and the Texas distinction among general, special, and quitclaim warranties.
key_takeaways: Dignity of calls (highest to lowest): natural monuments, artificial monuments, adjoinder, course, distance, quantity.
The hierarchy is a rule of construction that always yields to the intent of the parties and the footsteps of the original surveyor (Stafford v. King).
Texas narrows implied covenants (Prop. Code 5.023); a general warranty comes from the express 'warrant and forever defend' language.

content.html:
Why We Need Rules of Construction

Old deeds and grants are imperfect. A single description may call for a creek in one place and a bearing in another that do not agree; a distance may be short; a stated acreage may not match the geometry. Rules of construction are the legal canons courts and surveyors apply to decide which conflicting element of a description controls. The paramount rule is simple to state and hard to apply: give effect to the intent of the parties as expressed within the four corners of the instrument, read in light of the surrounding circumstances. Everything else is a tool to find that intent when the language fights itself.

The Dignity of Calls

When calls conflict, Texas ranks them by their relative reliability, or dignity. The presumption is that the more reliable, harder-to-mistake element better reflects what was actually run on the ground. From highest to lowest dignity:

  1. Calls for natural monuments / objects — rivers, creeks, lakes, mountains, established rocks.
  2. Calls for artificial monuments / objects — marked lines and trees, stakes, mounds, set iron, fences.
  3. Calls for adjoinder — an already-established adjacent survey line or boundary (inserted by modern Texas practice between artificial monuments and course).
  4. Course (bearing / direction).
  5. Distance.
  6. Quantity / area / acreage — the weakest call.

A found, identifiable monument on the ground generally controls over a conflicting bearing or distance, because the monument is physical evidence of where the line was actually located.

sections.title: Case: Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867)
sections.content: Stafford v. King is the foundational Texas authority on the dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine, and one of the most-cited 19th-century American statements of the rule. The Court explained that ‘natural objects are mountains, lakes, rivers, creeks, rocks, and the like’ while ‘artificial objects are marked lines, trees, stakes,’ and that ‘course and distance are the most unreliable calls’ — distance being less reliable than course — so that ‘course and distance yield to natural objects.’ Crucially, the Court framed the hierarchy conditionally: adopt the construction ‘most consistent with the intention apparent upon the face of the patent.’ Stafford also anchors the footsteps rule: ‘the actual identification of the survey, the footsteps of the surveyor upon the ground, should always be followed, by whatever rule they may be traced.’

content.text: Texas differs (emphasis): The dignity hierarchy is only a rebuttable rule of construction. It always yields to (1) the intent of the parties (paramount) and (2) the footsteps of the original surveyor where they can be ascertained on the ground. A lower-dignity call, even quantity, can control when the instrument and circumstances show that was the intent.

content.html:
Construing the Warranty: Three Texas Deeds

Deed construction also governs the strength of the title conveyed. Texas recognizes three principal forms:

  • General warranty deed — the grantor warrants and forever defends title against all defects, back through the entire chain of title.
  • Special warranty deed — the grantor warrants only against defects arising by, through, or under the grantor, not earlier links.
  • Quitclaim deed — conveys only whatever interest, if any, the grantor has; it carries no warranty and is not a muniment of title.

Under Tex. Property Code §5.022, the statutory warranty form uses ‘grant, sold, and conveyed’ and the operative ‘warrant and forever defend … against every person whomsoever lawfully claiming.’ A warranty is not required to make a valid conveyance.

content.text: Texas differs: At common law, 'grant' implied a broad covenant of seisin (that the grantor actually owns the estate). Tex. Property Code 5.023 deliberately narrows the implied covenants to just two: (1) the grantor has not previously conveyed the estate to another, and (2) the estate is free from encumbrances. It does NOT imply that the grantor actually owns the property. The general warranty comes from the express 'warrant and forever defend' language of 5.022.

sections.title: The Texas quitclaim wrinkle (and its 2021 softening)
sections.content: Classically, a grantee under a quitclaim is generally not a bona fide purchaser: the quitclaim is treated as notice that the grantor's title may be defective, so the grantee takes subject to prior outstanding claims (Woodward v. Ortiz, 237 S.W.2d 286 (Tex. 1951); Geodyne Energy v. Newton, 161 S.W.3d 482 (Tex. 2005)). Texas courts look at the granting language as a whole, not the label. 2026 update: Tex. Property Code §13.006 (eff. Sept. 1, 2021) creates a four-year cleansing window — after the fourth anniversary of recording, a quitclaim no longer affects a later purchaser's good faith and is not notice of unrecorded conveyances. The classic rule still binds the immediate grantee and applies during the first four years. Teach the two together.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Dignity of calls: natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoinder > course > distance > quantity.
The hierarchy is a rebuttable rule of construction; intent of the parties and the original surveyor's footsteps control over it (Stafford v. King).
General warranty defends against all defects; special warranty only against the grantor's own acts; quitclaim conveys only whatever interest exists.
Texas Property Code 5.023 narrows implied covenants to no-prior-conveyance and no-encumbrances; it does not imply ownership.
A quitclaim grantee is generally not a BFP, but 13.006 (2021) adds a four-year cleansing window for downstream purchasers.

flashcards.definition: The hierarchy ranking conflicting calls by reliability: natural monuments, artificial monuments, adjoinder, course, distance, and quantity (highest to lowest). A rebuttable rule of construction that yields to intent and the original surveyor's footsteps.

The foundational Texas case establishing the dignity-of-calls hierarchy and the footsteps doctrine, holding that course and distance yield to natural objects and that construction must follow the intent apparent on the face of the patent.

The cardinal Texas retracement principle: locate a boundary where the original surveyor actually ran and marked it, following that surveyor's footsteps where ascertainable, even over inconsistent field-note calls or later 'more accurate' measurements.

A deed conveying only whatever interest the grantor has, with no warranty; not a muniment of title. The grantee is generally not a bona fide purchaser, subject to the four-year cleansing window of Prop. Code 13.006 (2021).

lessons.title: Riparian and Littoral Rights
learning_objectives: Distinguish riparian rights (flowing watercourses) from littoral rights (static bodies) and their boundary consequences.
Apply the Texas gradient boundary method and the 30-foot statutory test for a navigable stream.
Explain how accretion, reliction, erosion, and avulsion move or fix a water boundary.
key_takeaways: Riparian land borders a flowing watercourse; littoral land borders a static body (lake, sea, Gulf).
On a navigable Texas stream the State owns the bed and the private boundary is the gradient boundary; a stream averaging 30 feet wide is navigable in law (Nat. Res. Code 21.001).
Gradual change (accretion/reliction/erosion) moves the boundary; sudden change (avulsion) fixes it.

content.html:
Riparian vs. Littoral

Land that touches water carries special boundary rules. Riparian rights attach to land bordering a flowing watercourse — a river or stream. Littoral rights attach to land bordering a static body — a lake, the sea, or the Gulf. The terminology controls which water-boundary rule applies. For a non-navigable stream, the riparian owner's title runs to the center thread of the channel. For a navigable stream, private title stops at the bank (located by the gradient boundary) and the State of Texas owns the bed, holding it in trust for the public. Littoral owners take to the relevant water line, with the bed below typically in the State on navigable and tidal waters.

The 30-Foot Navigability Test

Texas supplements the federal commerce-based navigability standard with a distinctive bright-line geometric test. Under Tex. Natural Resources Code §21.001(3), a ‘navigable stream’ is one that retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up. The 30-foot measurement is of the entire bed (between the banks), not the water flowing on any given day — so a channel that is dry much of the year can still be legally navigable, and therefore public, with a State-owned bed. The rule originates in an 1837 act of the Republic of Texas instructing surveyors not to cross such streams, carried forward into the modern code.

content.text: Texas differs: A small, non-commercial Texas creek can be legally 'navigable' purely because its bed averages 30 feet wide. This geometric test exists in essentially no other state, where navigability turns on navigability-in-fact for commerce. Always check bed width and the patent date before deciding who owns the bed.

content.html:
The Gradient Boundary Method

On a navigable Texas stream, the line dividing the State-owned bed from private upland is the gradient boundary — not the ordinary high-water mark used in most states. The gradient boundary is a graded line along the bank, located midway between (i) the lower water level that just reaches the cut bank and (ii) the higher level that just does not overtop the cut bank. It is tied to the ‘cut bank’ (the qualified bank) and refined in Brainard v. State as the ‘mid-height point of the lowest qualified bank in the vicinity.’ Locating it is specialized expert survey work — water-level observation, bank profiling, and gradient interpolation — not a simple high-water line.

sections.title: Key cases: gradient boundary
sections.content: Motl v. Boyd, 116 Tex. 82, 286 S.W. 458 (1926) — the Texas Supreme Court adopted the gradient boundary between public and private ownership along navigable streams and defined the ‘bed’ as soil alternately covered and left bare, adequate to contain the water at its average and mean stage during an entire year. Oklahoma v. Texas, 260 U.S. 606 (1923) — the U.S. Supreme Court adopted the gradient boundary for the Red River line; its surveyor Arthur A. Stiles developed the method. Brainard v. State, 12 S.W.3d 6 (Tex. 2000) — the leading modern application (Canadian River), restating and refining the location method.

sections.title: Coastal boundaries: the dual standard (Luttes v. State)
sections.content: On the Gulf coast the tidal boundary depends on the sovereign source of the grant. Under Luttes v. State, 159 Tex. 500, 324 S.W.2d 167 (1959), a Spanish or Mexican grant is bounded by the line of mean higher high water (the average of the higher of the two daily high tides over an ~18.6-year epoch), which pushes the boundary landward; an Anglo-American / Republic- or State-of-Texas grant is bounded by the line of mean high tide. A coastal surveyor must establish the chain of title back to the original sovereign before choosing the tidal datum.

content.html:
Moving Water, Moving Boundaries

Water lines are not static, and Texas follows the general doctrine keyed to gradual versus sudden change:

  • Accretion (gradual deposit of alluvion) and reliction (gradual uncovering of land by receding water): the boundary moves and the owner gains land.
  • Erosion (gradual loss): the boundary moves landward and the owner loses land.
  • Avulsion (a sudden, perceptible change — a flood cutting a new channel, a hurricane): the boundary is fixed at its former location and does not follow the new water line.

The distinctiveness in Texas is the line being measured: accretion and erosion shift the computed gradient boundary, not a simple high-water mark.

content.alt: Cross-section of a stream bank showing the gradient boundary located midway up the cut bank
content.caption: Figure: Gradient boundary located at the mid-height of the qualified (cut) bank of a navigable Texas stream, dividing State-owned bed from private upland.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Riparian = flowing watercourse; littoral = static body (lake, sea, Gulf).
Non-navigable stream: owner takes to the center thread. Navigable stream: State owns the bed; boundary is the gradient boundary.
A Texas stream averaging 30 feet of bed width is navigable in law (Nat. Res. Code 21.001), even if often dry.
The gradient boundary sits at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank (Motl v. Boyd; Brainard v. State).
Gradual change (accretion/reliction/erosion) moves the boundary; avulsion fixes it. Coastal datum depends on the sovereign source (Luttes v. State).

flashcards.definition: The Texas method for locating the line between a State-owned navigable streambed and private upland: a graded line at the mid-height of the qualified cut bank, midway between the level that just reaches and the level that just does not overtop the bank.
Under Tex. Nat. Res. Code 21.001(3), a stream that retains an average bed width of 30 feet from its mouth up. Such streams are public, with a State-owned bed, regardless of commercial navigability.

Riparian rights attach to land bordering a flowing watercourse (river/stream); littoral rights attach to land bordering a static body (lake, sea, Gulf). The label selects the applicable water-boundary rule.

Avulsion is a sudden, perceptible change in a watercourse; the boundary stays fixed at its former location. Accretion is gradual deposit; the boundary moves and the owner gains land. The controlling variable is gradual vs. sudden.

lessons.title: Adverse Possession and Acquiescence
learning_objectives: State the elements of adverse possession and the five Texas statutory limitation tracks (3/5/10/25/25 years).
Apply the distinctive Texas requirements: tax payment under the 5-year track and the 160-acre cap under the 10-year track.
Distinguish adverse possession from boundary by acquiescence and agreed boundary.
key_takeaways: Texas adverse possession is wholly statutory, in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16 (NOT the Property Code).
The five tracks: 16.024 (3 yr), 16.025 (5 yr + taxes), 16.026 (10 yr, 160-acre cap), 16.027 (25 yr), 16.028 (25 yr, void instrument).
Possession must be actual, visible, continuous, hostile, and under a claim of right for the full statutory period.

content.html:
What Adverse Possession Is

Adverse possession is the doctrine by which a person who occupies land belonging to another, openly and without permission, for a long enough period can acquire legal title by operation of a statute of limitations. In Texas it is defined as ‘an actual and visible appropriation of real property, commenced and continued under a claim of right that is inconsistent with and is hostile to the claim of another person’ (Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code §16.021). The general elements are possession that is actual, visible (open and notorious), continuous, exclusive, and hostile under a claim of right, for the entire statutory period. Permissive use never ripens into adverse possession.

content.text: Texas differs: Texas adverse possession is ENTIRELY statutory and lives in the Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Chapter 16 (Subchapter B), NOT in the Property Code. This is a common exam trap. Texas offers five distinct limitation tracks rather than the single common-law test.

content.html:
The Five Texas Tracks

Rather than one common-law standard, Texas provides five statutory limitation periods, each with its own elements. A claimant pleads under whichever period it can satisfy — the stronger the paper basis, the shorter the period. The strongest paper basis (title or color of title) perfects in just three years; bare possession with no instrument or taxes takes ten years and is capped at 160 acres.

rows: Peaceable & adverse possession under title or color of title (strongest paper basis)
Cultivate/use/enjoy + PAY taxes + claim under a duly registered deed (excludes quitclaim, forged deeds)
Peaceable & adverse, cultivating/using/enjoying; no instrument or tax needed but CAPPED AT 160 acres unless enclosed or under a recorded deed fixing boundaries
Runs regardless of a legal disability (minority, unsound mind); absolute long-stop
Under a recorded instrument purporting to convey, even if void on its face or in fact (excludes quitclaim/forged)

content.text: Texas differs (two distinctive features): (1) The 5-year track (16.025) REQUIRES paying the property taxes every year. A single missed year is fatal. (2) The 10-year track (16.026) is capped at 160 acres absent an enclosure or a recorded deed fixing the boundaries. Neither feature has a general common-law analog.

content.html:
Tacking

A claimant need not personally occupy the land for the entire statutory period. Under tacking, successive periods of adverse possession by different possessors may be added together if the possessors are in privity — connected by a deed, will, or other transfer of the possessory interest. Tacking lets a buyer add the seller's adverse-possession time to her own. But the statutory prerequisites still must be met throughout: under the 5-year track the taxes must be paid every year across the tacked periods, and the registered-deed or recorded-instrument requirement continues to apply. A gap in possession, or a break in privity, restarts the clock.

Acquiescence and Agreed Boundaries

Boundary by acquiescence and the agreed-boundary doctrine are related but distinct from adverse possession. They arise where adjoining owners, uncertain of the true line, treat a particular line (often a fence) as the boundary for a long period — or expressly agree to it — and a court then recognizes that line. Acquiescence does not transfer title by hostility and limitations; rather, it fixes the location of an uncertain boundary by the parties' long-continued conduct. Texas applies these doctrines narrowly: a fence built merely for convenience (a ‘fence of convenience’ to control livestock) does not, by itself, establish an agreed or acquiesced boundary, and the surveyor's measured location of the record line is usually dispositive unless a true dispute and recognition are proven.

content.text: Texas differs: Texas has no special 'innocent improver' / relative-hardship statute that automatically lets a good-faith encroacher buy the strip. Title passes only through the statutory adverse-possession tracks, and acquiescence/agreed-boundary doctrines are applied narrowly. The surveyor's measured boundary is typically controlling.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Adverse possession requires actual, visible, continuous, exclusive, hostile possession under a claim of right; permissive use never ripens.
Texas adverse possession is statutory, in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16 (16.021-16.028), not the Property Code.
Five tracks: 3-yr (16.024), 5-yr with taxes (16.025), 10-yr capped at 160 acres (16.026), 25-yr long-stop (16.027), 25-yr void instrument (16.028).
Tacking adds successive possessors' time if they are in privity and the statutory prerequisites are met throughout.
Acquiescence/agreed-boundary fix an uncertain line by long conduct; Texas applies them narrowly and a fence of convenience does not suffice.

flashcards.definition: Acquisition of title by actual, visible, continuous, exclusive, and hostile possession under a claim of right for a statutory period. Wholly statutory in Tex. Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16; permissive use never ripens.

Perfects adverse possession in 5 years if the claimant cultivates/uses/enjoys the land, claims under a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed, and PAYS the property taxes each year. A single missed tax year defeats the claim.
Under the 10-year track, bare adverse possession is limited to 160 acres unless a larger area is enclosed or the possession is under a recorded deed/memorandum fixing the boundaries.
flashcards.hint_1: Applies to the no-instrument, no-tax track.

flashcards.definition: A doctrine fixing the location of an uncertain boundary where adjoining owners long treat a particular line (often a fence) as the boundary. Distinct from adverse possession and applied narrowly in Texas; a fence of convenience does not suffice.

lessons.title: Easements and Rights-of-Way
learning_objectives: Define an easement and distinguish appurtenant from in-gross, and dominant from servient estates.
Identify the methods of creating easements in Texas (express, implied by prior use, by necessity, by prescription).
Explain why Texas favors express grants and disfavors prescriptive easements, and apply the strict-necessity rule.
key_takeaways: An easement is a non-possessory right to use another's land; appurtenant easements run with the dominant estate.
Texas strongly favors express, written easements and disfavors prescriptive easements (joint/shared use is fatal: Tran v. Macha).
Texas easement by necessity requires unity of ownership before severance, strict necessity, and necessity at severance; it ends when the necessity ends.

content.html:
What an Easement Is

An easement is a non-possessory right to use a portion of another person's land for a specified purpose — access, utilities, drainage, ingress and egress. The land burdened by the easement is the servient estate; if the easement benefits a particular parcel, that parcel is the dominant estate. An easement that benefits a parcel of land is appurtenant and runs with the dominant estate, passing automatically on conveyance. An easement that benefits a person or company regardless of land ownership (a pipeline or utility easement) is an easement in gross. A right-of-way is the common name for an easement of passage. Easements do not transfer ownership; they grant a use.

Creating Easements in Texas

Texas recognizes four principal routes to an easement, but the Texas courts strongly prefer the documented one:

  • Express easement — created by a written instrument satisfying the Statute of Frauds (Tex. Bus. & Com. Code §26.01). The most-favored and most common method.
  • Implied easement by prior use (quasi-easement) — requires unity of ownership before severance, a use that was open and apparent and continuous at severance, and that is reasonably necessary.
  • Easement by necessity — for landlocked access; requires strict necessity (below).
  • Prescriptive easement — acquired by long adverse use, analogous to adverse possession; disfavored in Texas (below).

content.text: Texas differs: Because Texas disfavors the implied and prescriptive routes, Texas access rights are far more often created by EXPRESS written grant than by operation of law. When researching access, look first for a recorded easement instrument.

content.html:
Easement by Necessity: Strict Necessity

An easement by necessity gives a landlocked owner access across the grantor's retained land. Texas requires all three elements: (1) unity of ownership of the dominant and servient estates as a single tract prior to severance; (2) access that is a present strict necessity, not a mere convenience; and (3) the necessity existed at the time of severance. The easement terminates when the necessity ends — for example, when alternative legal access becomes available. In Hamrick v. Ward, 446 S.W.3d 377 (Tex. 2014), the Texas Supreme Court held that a claim for roadway access to a landlocked, previously unified parcel must proceed under easement-by-necessity, not prior use — channeling access claims into the stricter framework.

sections.title: Why Texas disfavors prescriptive easements (Tran v. Macha)
sections.content: A prescriptive easement borrows the 10-year limitations period and requires use that is open and notorious, continuous and uninterrupted, adverse/hostile (permissive use never ripens), and exclusive. The decisive Texas requirement is exclusivity: ‘exclusive’ means use by the claimant and not by the landowner, so joint or shared use defeats the claim. In Tran v. Macha, 213 S.W.3d 913 (Tex. 2006), neighbors shared a driveway under a mutual mistake; the Texas Supreme Court reversed the easement, holding that mutual or joint use cannot support an exclusive, hostile claim. Under the general common-law / Restatement view, exclusivity is usually not required for prescription because an easement is non-possessory — so shared roadway use can create one elsewhere. Texas is the opposite: shared use is normally fatal, and Texas funnels access disputes toward necessity easements and express grants.

content.text: Texas differs (critical): Texas does NOT favor prescriptive easements and strictly scrutinizes every element. The key divergence is exclusivity: 'joint use of a road, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement.' Because most driveway/road-sharing fact patterns involve some owner use, Texas prescriptive claims rarely succeed (Tran v. Macha).

content.html:
The Surveyor's Role with Easements

Surveyors locate and depict easements, but the legal questions of validity belong to counsel. When mapping a tract, research recorded easement instruments and depict their location, width, and purpose; a 30-foot ingress-egress easement, a utility corridor, or a pipeline right-of-way must be shown because it burdens or benefits the survey. Compute the strip area of a rectangular easement as length times width. Flag apparent unrecorded uses (a visible road, a worn path, utility poles) as physical evidence that may indicate a claimed easement, but do not opine on whether a prescriptive or necessity easement exists — that is a legal conclusion for a court.

content.alt: Plan view of a dominant and servient estate with a rectangular access easement strip crossing the servient parcel
content.caption: Figure: An appurtenant access easement (right-of-way) burdening the servient estate and benefiting the dominant estate; strip area = length x width.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: An easement is a non-possessory right to use another's land; the burdened tract is servient, the benefited tract dominant.
Appurtenant easements run with the dominant estate; easements in gross benefit a person/company.
Texas creation routes: express (favored), implied by prior use, by necessity, by prescription (disfavored).
Easement by necessity requires unity of ownership before severance, strict necessity, and necessity at severance; it ends with the necessity (Hamrick v. Ward).
Texas disfavors prescriptive easements; joint/shared use is fatal to the exclusivity element (Tran v. Macha).

flashcards.definition: A non-possessory right to use another's land for a stated purpose. Appurtenant easements benefit a dominant estate and run with that land; easements in gross benefit a person or company regardless of land ownership.
The dominant estate is the parcel benefited by an appurtenant easement; the servient estate is the parcel burdened by it. Title remains with the servient owner; only a use is granted.

Access easement requiring (1) unity of ownership before severance, (2) strict necessity (not mere convenience), and (3) necessity existing at severance. It terminates when the necessity ends (Hamrick v. Ward).
flashcards.hint_1: Three elements; strict, not reasonable, necessity.

flashcards.definition: Texas requires a prescriptive easement to be 'exclusive' (used by the claimant and not the landowner). Joint or shared use, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement, so Texas disfavors and rarely grants them.

quiz_questions.question_text: Every privately held tract in Texas ultimately traces its title back to one of the 'sovereigns of the soil.' Which set correctly lists them?
options: The United States, the BLM, the federal GLO, and the State of Texas
Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, and the State of Texas
Spain, France, the Republic of Texas, and the United States
The Republic of Texas, the State of Texas, the county, and the GLO
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, and the State of Texas
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas titles originate in four sovereigns in order: Spain, Mexico, the Republic of Texas, and the State of Texas. The U.S./federal GLO/BLM were never the source of Texas grants because Texas retained its public lands at annexation.

quiz_questions.question_text: Because Texas land was never part of the federal public domain, Texas land is divided into PLSS townships, ranges, and sections.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: The opposite is true. Because Texas retained its public lands and was never federal public domain, there is no PLSS grid. Texas land is described by metes-and-bounds keyed to an original GLO survey and abstract number.

quiz_questions.question_text: A homeowner bolts a large standby generator to a concrete pad poured specifically to serve the house. In a boundary/title dispute, the generator is most likely classified as:
options: Personal property, because machinery is always a chattel
A fixture (real property), based on annexation, adaptation, and intention
An appurtenance that passes only if separately listed in the deed
A trade fixture the seller may always remove
quiz_questions.correct_answer: A fixture (real property), based on annexation, adaptation, and intention
quiz_questions.explanation: The generator is bolted down (annexation), serves the house's use (adaptation), and was installed with intent to make it permanent (intention, the controlling factor). It is a fixture and passes with the realty. Trade-fixture removal applies to tenant business installations, not this owner.

quiz_questions.question_text: An appurtenant easement benefiting Parcel A passes to a buyer of Parcel A only if the deed expressly lists it.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: An appurtenant easement is an appurtenance that runs with the dominant estate. It passes automatically with a conveyance of Parcel A even if the deed never mentions it.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed conveys land 'to the City so long as it is used as a public park.' What estate and future interest does this create?
options: Fee simple absolute, no future interest
Fee simple determinable, with a possibility of reverter in the grantor
Fee simple subject to condition subsequent, with a right of re-entry
Life estate, with a remainder in the public
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Fee simple determinable, with a possibility of reverter in the grantor
quiz_questions.explanation: The durational language 'so long as' creates a fee simple determinable that ends automatically if the condition fails, leaving the grantor a possibility of reverter. 'But if/provided that' language would instead create a condition subsequent with a right of re-entry.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Texas Property Code 5.001, what does a conveyance of real property pass when the deed uses no estate-limiting language?
options: A life estate, with a reversion to the grantor
The entire estate (a fee simple), unless a lesser estate is expressly limited
Only an easement to use the land
Nothing, because the estate must always be stated
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The entire estate (a fee simple), unless a lesser estate is expressly limited
quiz_questions.explanation: Property Code 5.001 presumes a conveyance passes the entire estate (a fee simple) unless the estate is limited by express words or a lesser estate is conveyed by construction or operation of law.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which archaic common-law rule did Texas Property Code 5.042 abolish?
options: The Rule in Shelley's Case
The doctrine of destructibility of contingent remainders
The recording act
The Statute of Frauds
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The Rule in Shelley's Case
quiz_questions.explanation: Section 5.042 abolished the Rule in Shelley's Case, the doctrine of worthier title, the rule against a remainder to the grantor's heirs, and a homestead-lien rule. It did NOT abolish destructibility of contingent remainders (Texas rejects that by case law).

quiz_questions.question_text: A Texas metes-and-bounds caption typically anchors the parcel to which locational reference?
options: A township, range, and section number
An original GLO survey name and abstract number
The nearest interstate highway mile marker
The federal BLM rectangular grid
quiz_questions.correct_answer: An original GLO survey name and abstract number
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas land is organized by original land-grant surveys, so a caption recites the survey (named for the grantee) and abstract number, e.g., 'the John Doe Survey, Abstract No. 123.' Township/range/section and the BLM grid belong to the PLSS, which Texas does not use.

quiz_questions.question_text: A description is so vague that the tract cannot be identified to the exclusion of other land. What is the legal consequence?
options: The description is cured automatically by the recording act
The description may be fatally defective under the Statute of Frauds, so the conveyance can fail
The surveyor may simply choose any reasonable boundary
The grantee automatically receives the entire county
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The description may be fatally defective under the Statute of Frauds, so the conveyance can fail
quiz_questions.explanation: To satisfy the Statute of Frauds a description must identify the land with enough certainty to locate it. A fatally vague description that cannot be made certain from the writing (or referenced writings) can void the conveyance.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a deed, a call for a creek conflicts with a call for a distance of 210 feet. Under the Texas dignity of calls, which controls absent contrary intent or footsteps evidence?
options: The distance, because numbers are precise
The creek (a natural monument), because natural objects outrank distance
The stated acreage, because quantity is most reliable
Whichever the current owner prefers
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The creek (a natural monument), because natural objects outrank distance
quiz_questions.explanation: Natural monuments sit at the top of the dignity hierarchy and control over course, distance, and quantity. Distance is among the least reliable calls; acreage/quantity is the weakest of all.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which Texas case is the foundational authority for the dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine?
options: Tran v. Macha
Stafford v. King
Motl v. Boyd
Madison v. Gordon
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Stafford v. King
quiz_questions.explanation: Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867), is the foundational Texas authority establishing the dignity hierarchy (natural over artificial over course/distance) and the rule that the original surveyor's footsteps should always be followed.

quiz_questions.question_text: The dignity-of-calls hierarchy is an absolute rule that can never be overridden by other evidence.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: The hierarchy is only a rebuttable rule of construction. It always yields to the intent of the parties (paramount) and to the footsteps of the original surveyor where ascertainable; even a lower-dignity call can control when intent so shows.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Texas Property Code 5.023, the words 'grant' or 'convey' in a deed imply which covenants?
options: A broad covenant of seisin that the grantor actually owns the estate
Only that the grantor has not previously conveyed the estate and that it is free from encumbrances
A warranty against all defects back through the entire chain of title
No covenants whatsoever
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Only that the grantor has not previously conveyed the estate and that it is free from encumbrances
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas 5.023 narrows the implied covenants to two: no prior conveyance and no encumbrances. Unlike common law, it does NOT imply seisin/ownership. A general warranty arises from the express 'warrant and forever defend' language of 5.022.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a NAVIGABLE Texas stream, who owns the bed and how is the private upland boundary located?
options: The abutting owner owns to the center thread; the boundary is the thread of the stream
The State owns the bed; the private boundary is the gradient boundary
The federal government owns the bed; the boundary is the ordinary high-water mark
Title is split 50/50; the boundary is the vegetation line
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The State owns the bed; the private boundary is the gradient boundary
quiz_questions.explanation: On a navigable stream the State of Texas owns the bed in trust for the public, and private title runs only to the gradient boundary (mid-height of the qualified cut bank). The center-thread rule applies to NON-navigable streams.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Tex. Natural Resources Code 21.001, a stream is 'navigable' if it retains an average bed width of how many feet from its mouth up?
quiz_questions.explanation: The statutory bright-line test defines a navigable stream as one that retains an average width of 30 feet from its mouth up, measured across the bed (not the water on a given day).

quiz_questions.question_text: A hurricane suddenly cuts a new channel across a riparian tract overnight. Under Texas water-boundary doctrine, what happens to the property boundary?
options: The boundary moves to follow the new channel (accretion)
The boundary is fixed at its former location because the change was avulsive
Title automatically passes to the State
The boundary disappears and the land becomes unowned
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The boundary is fixed at its former location because the change was avulsive
quiz_questions.explanation: Avulsion is a sudden, perceptible change; the boundary stays fixed at its former location and does not follow the new water line. Only gradual change (accretion/reliction/erosion) moves the boundary.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under Luttes v. State, the seaward boundary datum of a Texas coastal grant depends on the sovereign source: a Spanish/Mexican grant is bounded by mean higher high water.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: True
quiz_questions.explanation: Luttes v. State (1959) established the dual standard: Spanish/Mexican grants are bounded by mean higher high water (landward), while Anglo-American/Texas grants are bounded by mean high tide. The surveyor must establish the original sovereign first.

quiz_questions.question_text: In which Texas code does the law of adverse possession reside?
options: The Texas Property Code
The Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Chapter 16
The Texas Natural Resources Code
The Texas Occupations Code
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code, Chapter 16
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas adverse possession is wholly statutory and lives in Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16 (sections 16.021-16.028). A common trap is to look in the Property Code.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which distinctive requirement applies to the Texas 5-year adverse-possession track under section 16.025?
options: The claimant must enclose at least 640 acres
The claimant must pay the property taxes each year and claim under a duly registered deed
The claimant must obtain a court order before possession begins
The claimant must hold a quitclaim deed
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The claimant must pay the property taxes each year and claim under a duly registered deed
quiz_questions.explanation: The 5-year track requires cultivating/using/enjoying the land, claiming under a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed, AND paying the property taxes every year. A single missed tax year is fatal. Quitclaim and forged deeds are excluded.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under the 10-year adverse-possession track (16.026), bare possession with no instrument or taxes is generally capped at how many acres absent an enclosure or recorded deed fixing the boundaries?
quiz_questions.explanation: Section 16.026 caps a bare 10-year claim at 160 acres unless a larger area is enclosed or possession is under a recorded deed/memorandum fixing the boundaries.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two neighbors have shared a single gravel driveway for over 20 years, each using it freely. The claimant now seeks a prescriptive easement in Texas. What is the most likely outcome?
options: The easement is granted because 20 years exceeds the 10-year period
The claim fails because joint/shared use defeats the exclusivity requirement
The easement is granted automatically as an easement in gross
The claim succeeds because Texas favors prescriptive easements
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The claim fails because joint/shared use defeats the exclusivity requirement
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas requires a prescriptive easement to be exclusive (used by the claimant and not the owner). Per Tran v. Macha, joint or shared use, no matter how long, cannot ripen into a prescriptive easement. Texas disfavors prescriptive easements.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which combination is required for an easement by necessity in Texas?
options: Mere convenience, payment of taxes, and a recorded plat
Unity of ownership before severance, strict necessity, and necessity existing at severance
Twenty-five years of exclusive use and a quitclaim deed
Any landlocked condition arising at any later time
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Unity of ownership before severance, strict necessity, and necessity existing at severance
quiz_questions.explanation: Texas requires all three elements, and the easement ends when the necessity ends. Hamrick v. Ward channels landlocked roadway-access claims into easement-by-necessity rather than prior use.

quiz_questions.question_text: The land burdened by an easement is the dominant estate, and the land benefited is the servient estate.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.correct_answer: False
quiz_questions.explanation: The labels are reversed. The servient estate is burdened by the easement; the dominant estate is benefited by it. An appurtenant easement runs with the dominant estate.

problem_templates.name: Years to perfect Texas adverse possession (track selection)
problem_templates.question_template: A claimant has held peaceable and adverse possession of a Texas tract, paying all property taxes each year and claiming under a duly registered (non-quitclaim) deed, since {{startYear}}. Under the Texas 5-year limitation track (Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code 16.025), in what calendar year does the limitation period first ripen into perfected title?

solution_steps_template.title: Identify the track
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Registered deed + taxes paid each year = the 5-year track (16.025)

problem_templates.explanation_template: Because the claimant holds under a duly registered deed and pays taxes each year, the 5-year track applies. Title ripens 5 years after possession began: {{startYear}} + 5 = {{_answer}}.

problem_templates.name: Easement strip area (rectangular right-of-way)
problem_templates.question_template: A surveyor must compute the area of a straight rectangular access easement (right-of-way) that is {{width}} ft wide and {{length:f1}} ft long crossing the servient estate. What is the easement area, in acres? (1 acre = 43,560 sq ft.)
problem_templates.explanation_template: A rectangular easement strip has area = width × length = {{width}} × {{length:f1}} = {{areaSqFt:f1}} sq ft, which is {{areaSqFt:f1}} ÷ 43,560 = {{_answer}} acres.
